Complete Mathematical Formulations for Technical Paper¶

Detailed mathematical notation, prediction functions, loss functions, and algorithmic specifications for all models in the benchmark.

In [21]:
# Mathematical Formulas for Top Models - Technical Paper Documentation

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

print("="*100)
print("MATHEMATICAL FORMULATIONS FOR REGRESSION MODELS")
print("Complete formulas for technical paper - All models from benchmark")
print("="*100)

# Get top 10 models for detailed formulation
if 'results_df' in globals():
    top_models = results_df.head(10)['Model'].tolist()
else:
    top_models = []
    print("⚠️  Run results cells first to get model rankings")

print("\n" + "="*100)
print("SECTION 1: TOP PERFORMING MODELS - DETAILED MATHEMATICAL FORMULATION")
print("="*100)

# Helper function to get model hyperparameters
def get_model_params(model_name):
    trained_models_dict = {name: model for (name, model) in models if model is not None}
    model_obj = trained_models_dict.get(model_name)
    if model_obj:
        return model_obj.get_params()
    return {}

# ============================================================================
# 1. CatBoost Regressor
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "-"*100)
print("1. CATBOOST REGRESSOR (Categorical Boosting)")
print("-"*100)

catboost_params = get_model_params('CatBoostRegressor')

print("""
ALGORITHM: Gradient Boosting with Ordered Boosting (Yandex, 2018)

A. PREDICTION FUNCTION:

    ŷᵢ = F_M(xᵢ) = f₀ + Σ(m=1 to M) η · hₘ(xᵢ)

Where:
    • M = {iterations} (number of boosting iterations)
    • η = {learning_rate} (learning rate / shrinkage parameter)
    • hₘ(xᵢ) = m-th oblivious decision tree with depth {depth}
    • f₀ = initial prediction (typically mean of training targets)
    • xᵢ = feature vector (28 features in our case)

B. LOSS FUNCTION (RMSE):

    L = √(1/N Σ(i=1 to N) (yᵢ - ŷᵢ)²)

    Gradient: ∂L/∂ŷᵢ = -(yᵢ - ŷᵢ) / L

C. TREE STRUCTURE (Oblivious Trees):

    • Symmetric tree: All nodes at same level use same splitting feature
    • Depth = {depth}: Maximum tree depth (2^depth leaf nodes)
    • Each tree hₘ is trained to minimize residuals: rᵢ = yᵢ - Fₘ₋₁(xᵢ)

D. ORDERED BOOSTING:

    For each sample i, prediction shift is calculated as:
    
    sᵢ = E[hₘ(xᵢ) | trained on D \ Pᵢ]
    
    Where Pᵢ is permutation-dependent training set to reduce overfitting.

E. REGULARIZATION:

    L2 Leaf Regularization: λ = 3.0 (default)
    
    Leaf value w_j = -Σ(i∈Rⱼ) gᵢ / (Σ(i∈Rⱼ) hᵢ + λ)
    
    Where:
        • gᵢ = gradient at sample i
        • hᵢ = second derivative (Hessian) at sample i
        • Rⱼ = set of samples in leaf j

F. HYPERPARAMETERS USED:
""".format(
    iterations=catboost_params.get('iterations', 300),
    learning_rate=catboost_params.get('learning_rate', 0.05),
    depth=catboost_params.get('depth', 8)
))

if catboost_params:
    for param, value in catboost_params.items():
        if param in ['iterations', 'learning_rate', 'depth', 'l2_leaf_reg', 'bootstrap_type', 
                     'leaf_estimation_method', 'grow_policy', 'random_state']:
            print(f"    • {param:30s}: {value}")

print("""
G. KEY ALGORITHMIC FEATURES:

    1. Ordered Target Statistics: Reduces prediction shift by using different 
       permutations of training data
    
    2. Symmetric Trees: Binary trees where all nodes at same level share splitting feature
    
    3. Gradient-based Leaf Estimation: Uses Newton method for optimal leaf values
    
    4. Built-in Categorical Encoding: Not used here (all features are numeric)

CITATION:
    Prokhorenkova et al. (2018). CatBoost: unbiased boosting with categorical features.
    NeurIPS 2018, pp. 6638-6648.
""")

# ============================================================================
# 2. XGBoost
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "-"*100)
print("2. XGBOOST (Extreme Gradient Boosting)")
print("-"*100)

xgb_params = get_model_params('XGBRegressor')

print("""
ALGORITHM: Gradient Boosting with Regularization (Chen & Guestrin, 2016)

A. PREDICTION FUNCTION:

    ŷᵢ = Σ(k=1 to K) fₖ(xᵢ) = Σ(k=1 to K) wq(xᵢ)

Where:
    • K = {n_estimators} (number of trees)
    • fₖ represents k-th tree in additive ensemble
    • wq(xᵢ) = weight of leaf where sample xᵢ lands in tree k
    • q(xᵢ) maps sample to leaf index

B. OBJECTIVE FUNCTION:

    Obj(θ) = Σ(i=1 to N) l(yᵢ, ŷᵢ) + Σ(k=1 to K) Ω(fₖ)

Where:
    Loss: l(yᵢ, ŷᵢ) = (yᵢ - ŷᵢ)²  [Squared Error for regression]
    
    Regularization: Ω(fₖ) = γT + ½λΣ(j=1 to T) wⱼ²
    
        • T = number of leaves in tree k
        • γ = complexity penalty per leaf (default: 0)
        • λ = L2 regularization on weights (default: 1)
        • wⱼ = weight of leaf j

C. TREE BUILDING (Level-wise Growth):

    At iteration m, minimize:
    
    Objₘ = Σ(i=1 to N) [gᵢfₘ(xᵢ) + ½hᵢfₘ²(xᵢ)] + Ω(fₘ)
    
    Where:
        • gᵢ = ∂l/∂ŷᵢ (first derivative)
        • hᵢ = ∂²l/∂ŷᵢ² (second derivative)

D. SPLIT FINDING:

      
       Gain from split: Gain = ½[(Σ gₗ)²/(Σ hₗ + λ) + (Σ gᵣ)²/(Σ hᵣ + λ) - (Σ g)²/(Σ h + λ)] - γ
    
    Where L and R denote left and right child nodes.

E. HYPERPARAMETERS USED:
""".format(
    n_estimators=xgb_params.get('n_estimators', 300)
))

if xgb_params:
    for param, value in xgb_params.items():
        if param in ['n_estimators', 'learning_rate', 'max_depth', 'subsample', 
                     'colsample_bytree', 'gamma', 'lambda', 'alpha', 'min_child_weight']:
            print(f"    • {param:30s}: {value}")

print("""
F. KEY ALGORITHMIC FEATURES:

    1. Level-wise tree growth: Expands all nodes at same depth before next level
    2. Histogram-based split finding: Bins continuous features for efficiency
    3. Parallel tree building: Multi-threaded split search
    4. Built-in regularization: L1 (alpha) and L2 (lambda) on leaf weights

CITATION:
    Chen & Guestrin (2016). XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System.
    KDD 2016, pp. 785-794.
""")

# ============================================================================
# 3. LightGBM
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "-"*100)
print("3. LIGHTGBM (Light Gradient Boosting Machine)")
print("-"*100)

lgbm_params = get_model_params('LGBMRegressor')

lgbm_n_est = lgbm_params.get('n_estimators', 300)
lgbm_lr = lgbm_params.get('learning_rate', 0.05)
lgbm_leaves = lgbm_params.get('num_leaves', 31)
lgbm_samples = lgbm_params.get('min_child_samples', 20)

print(f"""
ALGORITHM: Gradient Boosting with Leaf-wise Growth (Microsoft, 2017)

A. PREDICTION FUNCTION:

    ŷᵢ = Σ(t=1 to T) ηfₜ(xᵢ)

Where:
    • T = {lgbm_n_est} (number of trees)
    • η = {lgbm_lr} (learning rate)
    • fₜ(xᵢ) = t-th decision tree output

B. LOSS FUNCTION:

    L = 1/(2N) Σ(i=1 to N) (yᵢ - ŷᵢ)²

C. LEAF-WISE TREE GROWTH:

    Unlike level-wise (XGBoost), LightGBM grows trees leaf-wise by choosing 
    the leaf with maximum delta loss:
    
    Δ_Loss = max_leaf [Gain(split) - λ]
    
    Where:
        Gain(split) = (Σ gₗ)²/(Σ hₗ + α) + (Σ gᵣ)²/(Σ hᵣ + α) - (Σ g)²/(Σ h + α)
    
    Constraints:
        • Max leaves = {lgbm_leaves}
        • Min samples per leaf = {lgbm_samples} (default: 20)

D. GRADIENT-BASED ONE-SIDE SAMPLING (GOSS):

    Keeps all large-gradient samples + random sample of small-gradient samples:
    
    GOSS(D) = {{{{xᵢ : |gᵢ| > threshold}}}} ∪ random_sample(D_small)
    
    Reduces computational cost while maintaining accuracy.

E. EXCLUSIVE FEATURE BUNDLING (EFB):

    Bundles mutually exclusive features (never take nonzero values simultaneously)
    to reduce feature dimension from d → d_bundle where d_bundle < d.

F. HYPERPARAMETERS USED:
""".format(
    n_estimators=lgbm_params.get('n_estimators', 300),
    learning_rate=lgbm_params.get('learning_rate', 0.05),
    num_leaves=lgbm_params.get('num_leaves', 31),
    min_child_samples=lgbm_params.get('min_child_samples', 20)
))

if lgbm_params:
    for param, value in lgbm_params.items():
        if param in ['n_estimators', 'learning_rate', 'num_leaves', 'max_depth', 
                     'subsample', 'colsample_bytree', 'min_child_samples', 'reg_alpha', 'reg_lambda']:
            print(f"    • {param:30s}: {value}")

print("""
G. KEY ALGORITHMIC FEATURES:

    1. Leaf-wise growth: Faster convergence than level-wise
    2. Histogram-based: Bins features into discrete bins (default: 255)
    3. GOSS: Gradient-based sampling reduces data size
    4. EFB: Feature bundling reduces dimensionality
    5. Categorical support: Native handling of categorical features

CITATION:
    Ke et al. (2017). LightGBM: A Highly Efficient Gradient Boosting Decision Tree.
    NeurIPS 2017, pp. 3146-3154.
""")

# ============================================================================
# 4. Gradient Boosting Regressor (sklearn)
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "-"*100)
print("4. GRADIENT BOOSTING REGRESSOR (Scikit-learn)")
print("-"*100)

gb_params = get_model_params('GradientBoostingRegressor')

n_est = gb_params.get('n_estimators', 200)
lr = gb_params.get('learning_rate', 0.1)
md = gb_params.get('max_depth', 3)
ss = gb_params.get('subsample', 1.0)

print(f"""
ALGORITHM: Classic Gradient Boosting (Friedman, 2001)

A. PREDICTION FUNCTION:

    F_M(x) = f₀(x) + Σ(m=1 to M) η · hₘ(x)

Where:
    • M = {n_est}
    • η = {lr}
    • hₘ(x) = m-th regression tree (depth = {md})
    • f₀(x) = initial prediction (mean of y)

B. SEQUENTIAL TREE FITTING:

    At iteration m:
    
    1. Compute pseudo-residuals: rᵢₘ = -[∂L(yᵢ, F(xᵢ))/∂F(xᵢ)]|_F=Fₘ₋₁
    
       For squared error: rᵢₘ = yᵢ - Fₘ₋₁(xᵢ)
    
    2. Fit regression tree hₘ to residuals {{(xᵢ, rᵢₘ)}}ᵢ₌₁ᴺ
    
    3. Update: Fₘ(x) = Fₘ₋₁(x) + η · hₘ(x)

C. LOSS FUNCTION:

    L = 1/N Σ(i=1 to N) (yᵢ - F(xᵢ))²

D. SUBSAMPLE (STOCHASTIC GB):

    Each tree trained on random subsample: {ss} × N samples
    
    Reduces variance and prevents overfitting.

E. HYPERPARAMETERS USED:
""")

if gb_params:
    for param, value in gb_params.items():
        if param in ['n_estimators', 'learning_rate', 'max_depth', 'subsample', 
                     'min_samples_split', 'min_samples_leaf', 'max_features']:
            print(f"    • {param:30s}: {value}")

print("""
F. KEY FEATURES:

    1. Functional gradient descent in function space
    2. Forward stagewise additive modeling
    3. Shrinkage (learning rate) prevents overfitting
    4. Stochastic gradient boosting via subsampling

CITATION:
    Friedman (2001). Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine.
    Annals of Statistics, 29(5), 1189-1232.
""")

# ============================================================================
# 5. Random Forest
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "-"*100)
print("5. RANDOM FOREST REGRESSOR")
print("-"*100)

rf_params = get_model_params('RandomForestRegressor')

# Use regular string concatenation instead of f-string for mathematical formulas
iterations_val = rf_params.get('n_estimators', 200)
max_features_val = rf_params.get('max_features', 'auto')

print(f"""
ALGORITHM: Bootstrap Aggregating (Bagging) with Random Feature Selection

A. PREDICTION FUNCTION (Ensemble Average):

    F(x) = 1/B Σ(b=1 to B) fᵦ(x)

Where:
    • B = {iterations_val} (number of trees)
    • fᵦ(x) = b-th decision tree trained on bootstrap sample Dᵦ

B. BOOTSTRAP SAMPLING:

    For each tree b:
    
    1. Draw bootstrap sample: Dᵦ = {{(xᵢ, yᵢ)}}ᵢ~Uniform({{1,...,N}})
       (sampling with replacement, typically ~63.2% unique samples)
    
    2. Train tree fᵦ on Dᵦ with random feature selection at each split

C. RANDOM FEATURE SELECTION:

    At each node split, consider random subset of features:
    
    m_try = {max_features_val}  (number of features to consider)
    
    Default: m_try = √d for classification, d/3 for regression (d = total features)

D. TREE SPLITTING CRITERION:

    Mean Squared Error (MSE) reduction:
    
    MSE(node) = 1/|Rⱼ| Σ(i∈Rⱼ) (yᵢ - ȳⱼ)²
    
    Where ȳⱼ = mean of samples in node j

E. HYPERPARAMETERS USED:
""")

if rf_params:
    for param, value in rf_params.items():
        if param in ['n_estimators', 'max_depth', 'min_samples_split', 'min_samples_leaf', 'max_features']:
            print(f"    • {param:30s}: {value}")

print("""
F. KEY FEATURES:

    1. Ensemble of decision trees (typically 100s of trees)
    2. Each tree trained on random subset of data + features
    3. Reduces overfitting compared to individual trees
    4. Feature importance via mean decrease impurity

CITATION:
    Breiman (2001). Random Forests. Machine Learning, 45(1), 5-32.
""")

# ============================================================================
# 6-10: Summary of Other Top Models
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*100)
print("SECTION 2: MATHEMATICAL FORMULATIONS FOR OTHER TOP MODELS")
print("="*100)

# Ridge Regression
print("\n" + "-"*100)
print("6. RIDGE REGRESSION (L2 Regularization)")
print("-"*100)

ridge_params = get_model_params('Ridge')

print("""
PREDICTION FUNCTION:
    ŷ = Xβ + β₀

LOSS FUNCTION (L2-Penalized Least Squares):
    L(β) = ||y - Xβ||² + α||β||²₂
         = Σ(yᵢ - xᵢᵀβ)² + α Σβⱼ²

ANALYTICAL SOLUTION:
    β̂ = (XᵀX + αI)⁻¹Xᵀy

HYPERPARAMETERS:
    • α = {alpha} (regularization strength)

CITATION: Hoerl & Kennard (1970). Ridge Regression: Biased Estimation for Nonorthogonal Problems.
""".format(alpha=ridge_params.get('alpha', 1.0)))

# Lasso Regression
print("\n" + "-"*100)
print("7. LASSO REGRESSION (L1 Regularization)")
print("-"*100)

lasso_params = get_model_params('Lasso')

print("""
PREDICTION FUNCTION:
    ŷ = Xβ + β₀

LOSS FUNCTION (L1-Penalized Least Squares):
    L(β) = 1/(2N) ||y - Xβ||² + α||β||₁
         = 1/(2N) Σ(yᵢ - xᵢᵀβ)² + α Σ|βⱼ|

OPTIMIZATION (Coordinate Descent):
    No closed-form solution; solved iteratively via:
    
    β̂ⱼ = S(XⱼᵀRⱼ, Nα) / (XⱼᵀXⱼ)
    
    Where S(z, λ) = sign(z)(|z| - λ)₊ (soft thresholding)

FEATURE SELECTION:
    • Induces sparsity: some β̂ⱼ = 0 exactly
    • Automatic feature selection property

HYPERPARAMETERS:
    • α = {alpha} (regularization strength)

CITATION: Tibshirani (1996). Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso.
""".format(alpha=lasso_params.get('alpha', 1.0)))

# ElasticNet
print("\n" + "-"*100)
print("8. ELASTIC NET (L1 + L2 Regularization)")
print("-"*100)

elastic_params = get_model_params('ElasticNet')

print("""
PREDICTION FUNCTION:
    ŷ = Xβ + β₀

LOSS FUNCTION (Combined L1/L2 Penalty):
    L(β) = 1/(2N) ||y - Xβ||² + α[ρ||β||₁ + (1-ρ)/2 ||β||₂²]
    
    Where:
        • ρ = {l1_ratio} (L1 ratio: 0=Ridge, 1=Lasso)
        • α = {alpha} (overall regularization strength)

OPTIMIZATION:
    Coordinate descent with elastic penalty.

ADVANTAGES:
    • Combines feature selection (L1) and coefficient shrinkage (L2)
    • Handles correlated features better than Lasso
    • More stable than Lasso when p >> n

HYPERPARAMETERS:
    • α = {alpha}
    • l1_ratio = {l1_ratio}

CITATION: Zou & Hastie (2005). Regularization and Variable Selection via the Elastic Net.
""".format(alpha=elastic_params.get('alpha', 1.0), l1_ratio=elastic_params.get('l1_ratio', 0.5)))

# SVR
print("\n" + "-"*100)
print("9. SUPPORT VECTOR REGRESSION (SVR)")
print("-"*100)

svr_params = get_model_params('SVR')

print("""
PREDICTION FUNCTION (Kernel Trick):
    f(x) = Σ(i=1 to N) (αᵢ - αᵢ*)K(xᵢ, x) + b

Where:
    • K(xᵢ, x) = kernel function (RBF kernel used)
    • αᵢ, αᵢ* = dual variables (Lagrange multipliers)
    • b = bias term

KERNEL FUNCTION (RBF):
    K(xᵢ, xⱼ) = exp(-γ||xᵢ - xⱼ||²)
    
    Where γ = {gamma}

LOSS FUNCTION (ε-insensitive):
    L_ε(y, f(x)) = max(0, |y - f(x)| - ε)
    
    No penalty for errors < ε (defines tube around regression line)

OPTIMIZATION (Primal Form):
    min  1/2||w||² + C Σ(ξᵢ + ξᵢ*)
    s.t. |yᵢ - f(xᵢ)| ≤ ε + ξᵢ

HYPERPARAMETERS:
    • C = {C} (regularization parameter)
    • γ = {gamma} (kernel coefficient)
    • ε = {epsilon} (epsilon in epsilon-SVR)

CITATION: Vapnik (1995). The Nature of Statistical Learning Theory.
""".format(
    C=svr_params.get('C', 1.0),
    gamma=svr_params.get('gamma', 'scale'),
    epsilon=svr_params.get('epsilon', 0.1)
))

# KNN
print("\n" + "-"*100)
print("10. K-NEAREST NEIGHBORS REGRESSOR")
print("-"*100)

knn_params = get_model_params('KNeighborsRegressor')

print("""
PREDICTION FUNCTION (Non-parametric):
    ŷ(x) = 1/K Σ(xᵢ∈N_K(x)) yᵢ

Where:
    • N_K(x) = K nearest neighbors of x in training set
    • K = {n_neighbors}
    • Distance metric: Euclidean d(xᵢ, xⱼ) = ||xᵢ - xⱼ||₂

WEIGHTED VARIANT:
    ŷ(x) = Σ(i=1 to K) wᵢyᵢ / Σ(i=1 to K) wᵢ
    
    Where wᵢ = 1/d(x, xᵢ) (inverse distance weighting)

COMPLEXITY:
    • Training: O(1) (stores all training data)
    • Prediction: O(Nd) for each query (can use KD-tree for O(log N))

HYPERPARAMETERS:
    • n_neighbors = {n_neighbors}
    • weights = '{weights}' (uniform or distance)
    • metric = 'euclidean'

CITATION: Fix & Hodges (1951). Discriminatory Analysis - Nonparametric Discrimination.
""".format(
    n_neighbors=knn_params.get('n_neighbors', 5),
    weights=knn_params.get('weights', 'uniform')
))

# ============================================================================
# General Notation Reference
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*100)
print("SECTION 3: NOTATION REFERENCE")
print("="*100)

print("""
COMMON NOTATION USED ACROSS ALL MODELS:

VARIABLES:
    • N = number of training samples = {N_train}
    • d = number of features = {d_features}
    • xᵢ ∈ ℝᵈ = feature vector for sample i
    • yᵢ ∈ ℝ = target value for sample i
    • ŷᵢ = predicted value for sample i
    • X = [x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ]ᵀ ∈ ℝᴺˣᵈ = design matrix
    • y = [y₁, y₂, ..., yₙ]ᵀ ∈ ℝᴺ = target vector
    • β ∈ ℝᵈ = coefficient vector
    • β₀ ∈ ℝ = intercept term

LOSS FUNCTIONS:
    • MSE = 1/N Σ(yᵢ - ŷᵢ)² (Mean Squared Error)
    • RMSE = √MSE (Root Mean Squared Error)
    • MAE = 1/N Σ|yᵢ - ŷᵢ| (Mean Absolute Error)
    • R² = 1 - SS_res/SS_tot (Coefficient of Determination)
        Where:
            SS_res = Σ(yᵢ - ŷᵢ)² (residual sum of squares)
            SS_tot = Σ(yᵢ - ȳ)² (total sum of squares)

REGULARIZATION:
    • L1 norm: ||β||₁ = Σ|βⱼ| (Lasso penalty)
    • L2 norm: ||β||₂² = Σβⱼ² (Ridge penalty)
    • α, λ = regularization strength hyperparameters

GRADIENTS:
    • gᵢ = ∂L/∂ŷᵢ (first-order gradient)
    • hᵢ = ∂²L/∂ŷᵢ² (second-order gradient / Hessian)

ENSEMBLE NOTATION:
    • M, B, T, K = number of base models/trees
    • η = learning rate / shrinkage parameter
    • fₖ(x) = k-th base model output
    • wⱼ = leaf weight in tree-based models

EVALUATION METRICS REPORTED:
    • Test R² = {best_r2:.4f} (best model)
    • Test RMSE = {best_rmse:.2f} (best model)
    • Test MAE = {best_mae:.2f} (best model)

DATA SPLIT:
    • Training: 60% = {N_train} samples
    • Validation: 20% = {N_val} samples
    • Testing: 20% = {N_test} samples
""".format(
    N_train=len(X_train) if 'X_train' in globals() else 'N/A',
    d_features=X_train.shape[1] if 'X_train' in globals() else 28,
    best_r2=results_df.iloc[0]['R2'] if 'results_df' in globals() else 0,
    best_rmse=results_df.iloc[0]['RMSE'] if 'results_df' in globals() else 0,
    best_mae=results_df.iloc[0]['MAE'] if 'results_df' in globals() else 0,
    N_val=len(X_val) if 'X_val' in globals() else 'N/A',
    N_test=len(X_test) if 'X_test' in globals() else 'N/A'
))

# ============================================================================
# Export Summary Table
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*100)
print("SECTION 4: MODEL FAMILY SUMMARY TABLE")
print("="*100)

model_families = {
    'Gradient Boosting': ['CatBoostRegressor', 'XGBRegressor', 'LGBMRegressor', 'GradientBoostingRegressor', 'AdaBoostRegressor'],
    'Random Forest': ['RandomForestRegressor', 'ExtraTreesRegressor'],
    'Linear Models': ['Ridge', 'Lasso', 'ElasticNet', 'LinearRegression', 'BayesianRidge', 'Lars', 'LassoLars'],
    'Kernel Methods': ['SVR', 'KernelRidge', 'GaussianProcessRegressor'],
    'Instance-based': ['KNeighborsRegressor'],
    'Tree-based': ['DecisionTreeRegressor']
}

family_summary = []
for family, models_list in model_families.items():
    if 'results_df' in globals():
        family_models = results_df[results_df['Model'].isin(models_list)]
        if not family_models.empty:
            family_summary.append({
                'Model Family': family,
                'Count': len(family_models),
                'Best R²': family_models['R2'].max(),
                'Mean R²': family_models['R2'].mean(),
                'Std R²': family_models['R2'].std(),
                'Representative Model': family_models.iloc[0]['Model']
            })

family_df = pd.DataFrame(family_summary)
if not family_df.empty:
    family_df = family_df.sort_values('Best R²', ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)
    print("\nModel Family Performance Summary:\n")
    print(family_df.to_string(index=False, float_format=lambda x: f'{x:.4f}'))
    
    family_df.to_csv('table_model_family_mathematical_summary.csv', index=False)
    print("\n✅ Saved: table_model_family_mathematical_summary.csv")

print("\n" + "="*100)
print("✅ MATHEMATICAL FORMULATION DOCUMENTATION COMPLETE")
print("="*100)

print("""
FILES GENERATED FOR TECHNICAL PAPER:
    • Console output above contains complete formulations
    • table_model_family_mathematical_summary.csv (family comparison)

RECOMMENDED USAGE IN PAPER:
    1. Methods Section: Include prediction functions + loss functions
    2. Appendix: Full algorithmic details (ordered boosting, leaf-wise growth, etc.)
    3. Tables: Hyperparameter values for reproducibility
    4. Notation: Reference Section 3 for symbol definitions

KEY CITATIONS TO INCLUDE:
    • CatBoost: Prokhorenkova et al. (2018) NeurIPS
    • XGBoost: Chen & Guestrin (2016) KDD
    • LightGBM: Ke et al. (2017) NeurIPS
    • Gradient Boosting: Friedman (2001) Annals of Statistics
    • Random Forest: Breiman (2001) Machine Learning
    • Lasso: Tibshirani (1996) JRSSB
    • Elastic Net: Zou & Hastie (2005) JRSSB
    • SVR: Vapnik (1995) Springer
""")

====================================================================================================
MATHEMATICAL FORMULATIONS FOR REGRESSION MODELS
Complete formulas for technical paper - All models from benchmark
====================================================================================================

====================================================================================================
SECTION 1: TOP PERFORMING MODELS - DETAILED MATHEMATICAL FORMULATION
====================================================================================================

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. CATBOOST REGRESSOR (Categorical Boosting)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ALGORITHM: Gradient Boosting with Ordered Boosting (Yandex, 2018)

A. PREDICTION FUNCTION:

    ŷᵢ = F_M(xᵢ) = f₀ + Σ(m=1 to M) η · hₘ(xᵢ)

Where:
    • M = 300 (number of boosting iterations)
    • η = 0.05 (learning rate / shrinkage parameter)
    • hₘ(xᵢ) = m-th oblivious decision tree with depth 8
    • f₀ = initial prediction (typically mean of training targets)
    • xᵢ = feature vector (28 features in our case)

B. LOSS FUNCTION (RMSE):

    L = √(1/N Σ(i=1 to N) (yᵢ - ŷᵢ)²)

    Gradient: ∂L/∂ŷᵢ = -(yᵢ - ŷᵢ) / L

C. TREE STRUCTURE (Oblivious Trees):

    • Symmetric tree: All nodes at same level use same splitting feature
    • Depth = 8: Maximum tree depth (2^depth leaf nodes)
    • Each tree hₘ is trained to minimize residuals: rᵢ = yᵢ - Fₘ₋₁(xᵢ)

D. ORDERED BOOSTING:

    For each sample i, prediction shift is calculated as:

    sᵢ = E[hₘ(xᵢ) | trained on D \ Pᵢ]

    Where Pᵢ is permutation-dependent training set to reduce overfitting.

E. REGULARIZATION:

    L2 Leaf Regularization: λ = 3.0 (default)

    Leaf value w_j = -Σ(i∈Rⱼ) gᵢ / (Σ(i∈Rⱼ) hᵢ + λ)

    Where:
        • gᵢ = gradient at sample i
        • hᵢ = second derivative (Hessian) at sample i
        • Rⱼ = set of samples in leaf j

F. HYPERPARAMETERS USED:

    • iterations                    : 300
    • learning_rate                 : 0.05
    • depth                         : 8
    • random_state                  : 42

G. KEY ALGORITHMIC FEATURES:

    1. Ordered Target Statistics: Reduces prediction shift by using different 
       permutations of training data

    2. Symmetric Trees: Binary trees where all nodes at same level share splitting feature

    3. Gradient-based Leaf Estimation: Uses Newton method for optimal leaf values

    4. Built-in Categorical Encoding: Not used here (all features are numeric)

CITATION:
    Prokhorenkova et al. (2018). CatBoost: unbiased boosting with categorical features.
    NeurIPS 2018, pp. 6638-6648.


----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. XGBOOST (Extreme Gradient Boosting)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ALGORITHM: Gradient Boosting with Regularization (Chen & Guestrin, 2016)

A. PREDICTION FUNCTION:

    ŷᵢ = Σ(k=1 to K) fₖ(xᵢ) = Σ(k=1 to K) wq(xᵢ)

Where:
    • K = 300 (number of trees)
    • fₖ represents k-th tree in additive ensemble
    • wq(xᵢ) = weight of leaf where sample xᵢ lands in tree k
    • q(xᵢ) maps sample to leaf index

B. OBJECTIVE FUNCTION:

    Obj(θ) = Σ(i=1 to N) l(yᵢ, ŷᵢ) + Σ(k=1 to K) Ω(fₖ)

Where:
    Loss: l(yᵢ, ŷᵢ) = (yᵢ - ŷᵢ)²  [Squared Error for regression]

    Regularization: Ω(fₖ) = γT + ½λΣ(j=1 to T) wⱼ²

        • T = number of leaves in tree k
        • γ = complexity penalty per leaf (default: 0)
        • λ = L2 regularization on weights (default: 1)
        • wⱼ = weight of leaf j

C. TREE BUILDING (Level-wise Growth):

    At iteration m, minimize:

    Objₘ = Σ(i=1 to N) [gᵢfₘ(xᵢ) + ½hᵢfₘ²(xᵢ)] + Ω(fₘ)

    Where:
        • gᵢ = ∂l/∂ŷᵢ (first derivative)
        • hᵢ = ∂²l/∂ŷᵢ² (second derivative)

D. SPLIT FINDING:


       Gain from split: Gain = ½[(Σ gₗ)²/(Σ hₗ + λ) + (Σ gᵣ)²/(Σ hᵣ + λ) - (Σ g)²/(Σ h + λ)] - γ

    Where L and R denote left and right child nodes.

E. HYPERPARAMETERS USED:

    • colsample_bytree              : None
    • gamma                         : None
    • learning_rate                 : 0.05
    • max_depth                     : 7
    • min_child_weight              : None
    • n_estimators                  : 300
    • subsample                     : None

F. KEY ALGORITHMIC FEATURES:

    1. Level-wise tree growth: Expands all nodes at same depth before next level
    2. Histogram-based split finding: Bins continuous features for efficiency
    3. Parallel tree building: Multi-threaded split search
    4. Built-in regularization: L1 (alpha) and L2 (lambda) on leaf weights

CITATION:
    Chen & Guestrin (2016). XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System.
    KDD 2016, pp. 785-794.


----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. LIGHTGBM (Light Gradient Boosting Machine)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ALGORITHM: Gradient Boosting with Leaf-wise Growth (Microsoft, 2017)

A. PREDICTION FUNCTION:

    ŷᵢ = Σ(t=1 to T) ηfₜ(xᵢ)

Where:
    • T = 300 (number of trees)
    • η = 0.05 (learning rate)
    • fₜ(xᵢ) = t-th decision tree output

B. LOSS FUNCTION:

    L = 1/(2N) Σ(i=1 to N) (yᵢ - ŷᵢ)²

C. LEAF-WISE TREE GROWTH:

    Unlike level-wise (XGBoost), LightGBM grows trees leaf-wise by choosing 
    the leaf with maximum delta loss:

    Δ_Loss = max_leaf [Gain(split) - λ]

    Where:
        Gain(split) = (Σ gₗ)²/(Σ hₗ + α) + (Σ gᵣ)²/(Σ hᵣ + α) - (Σ g)²/(Σ h + α)

    Constraints:
        • Max leaves = 50
        • Min samples per leaf = 20 (default: 20)

D. GRADIENT-BASED ONE-SIDE SAMPLING (GOSS):

    Keeps all large-gradient samples + random sample of small-gradient samples:

    GOSS(D) = {xᵢ : |gᵢ| > threshold} ∪ random_sample(D_small)

    Reduces computational cost while maintaining accuracy.

E. EXCLUSIVE FEATURE BUNDLING (EFB):

    Bundles mutually exclusive features (never take nonzero values simultaneously)
    to reduce feature dimension from d → d_bundle where d_bundle < d.

F. HYPERPARAMETERS USED:

    • colsample_bytree              : 1.0
    • learning_rate                 : 0.05
    • max_depth                     : -1
    • min_child_samples             : 20
    • n_estimators                  : 300
    • num_leaves                    : 50
    • reg_alpha                     : 0.0
    • reg_lambda                    : 0.0
    • subsample                     : 1.0

G. KEY ALGORITHMIC FEATURES:

    1. Leaf-wise growth: Faster convergence than level-wise
    2. Histogram-based: Bins features into discrete bins (default: 255)
    3. GOSS: Gradient-based sampling reduces data size
    4. EFB: Feature bundling reduces dimensionality
    5. Categorical support: Native handling of categorical features

CITATION:
    Ke et al. (2017). LightGBM: A Highly Efficient Gradient Boosting Decision Tree.
    NeurIPS 2017, pp. 3146-3154.


----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. GRADIENT BOOSTING REGRESSOR (Scikit-learn)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ALGORITHM: Classic Gradient Boosting (Friedman, 2001)

A. PREDICTION FUNCTION:

    F_M(x) = f₀(x) + Σ(m=1 to M) η · hₘ(x)

Where:
    • M = 200
    • η = 0.1
    • hₘ(x) = m-th regression tree (depth = 5)
    • f₀(x) = initial prediction (mean of y)

B. SEQUENTIAL TREE FITTING:

    At iteration m:

    1. Compute pseudo-residuals: rᵢₘ = -[∂L(yᵢ, F(xᵢ))/∂F(xᵢ)]|_F=Fₘ₋₁

       For squared error: rᵢₘ = yᵢ - Fₘ₋₁(xᵢ)

    2. Fit regression tree hₘ to residuals {(xᵢ, rᵢₘ)}ᵢ₌₁ᴺ

    3. Update: Fₘ(x) = Fₘ₋₁(x) + η · hₘ(x)

C. LOSS FUNCTION:

    L = 1/N Σ(i=1 to N) (yᵢ - F(xᵢ))²

D. SUBSAMPLE (STOCHASTIC GB):

    Each tree trained on random subsample: 1.0 × N samples

    Reduces variance and prevents overfitting.

E. HYPERPARAMETERS USED:

    • learning_rate                 : 0.1
    • max_depth                     : 5
    • max_features                  : None
    • min_samples_leaf              : 1
    • min_samples_split             : 2
    • n_estimators                  : 200
    • subsample                     : 1.0

F. KEY FEATURES:

    1. Functional gradient descent in function space
    2. Forward stagewise additive modeling
    3. Shrinkage (learning rate) prevents overfitting
    4. Stochastic gradient boosting via subsampling

CITATION:
    Friedman (2001). Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine.
    Annals of Statistics, 29(5), 1189-1232.


----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. RANDOM FOREST REGRESSOR
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ALGORITHM: Bootstrap Aggregating (Bagging) with Random Feature Selection

A. PREDICTION FUNCTION (Ensemble Average):

    F(x) = 1/B Σ(b=1 to B) fᵦ(x)

Where:
    • B = 200 (number of trees)
    • fᵦ(x) = b-th decision tree trained on bootstrap sample Dᵦ

B. BOOTSTRAP SAMPLING:

    For each tree b:

    1. Draw bootstrap sample: Dᵦ = {(xᵢ, yᵢ)}ᵢ~Uniform({1,...,N})
       (sampling with replacement, typically ~63.2% unique samples)

    2. Train tree fᵦ on Dᵦ with random feature selection at each split

C. RANDOM FEATURE SELECTION:

    At each node split, consider random subset of features:

    m_try = 1.0  (number of features to consider)

    Default: m_try = √d for classification, d/3 for regression (d = total features)

D. TREE SPLITTING CRITERION:

    Mean Squared Error (MSE) reduction:

    MSE(node) = 1/|Rⱼ| Σ(i∈Rⱼ) (yᵢ - ȳⱼ)²

    Where ȳⱼ = mean of samples in node j

E. HYPERPARAMETERS USED:

    • max_depth                     : 15
    • max_features                  : 1.0
    • min_samples_leaf              : 1
    • min_samples_split             : 2
    • n_estimators                  : 200

F. KEY FEATURES:

    1. Ensemble of decision trees (typically 100s of trees)
    2. Each tree trained on random subset of data + features
    3. Reduces overfitting compared to individual trees
    4. Feature importance via mean decrease impurity

CITATION:
    Breiman (2001). Random Forests. Machine Learning, 45(1), 5-32.


====================================================================================================
SECTION 2: MATHEMATICAL FORMULATIONS FOR OTHER TOP MODELS
====================================================================================================

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. RIDGE REGRESSION (L2 Regularization)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PREDICTION FUNCTION:
    ŷ = Xβ + β₀

LOSS FUNCTION (L2-Penalized Least Squares):
    L(β) = ||y - Xβ||² + α||β||²₂
         = Σ(yᵢ - xᵢᵀβ)² + α Σβⱼ²

ANALYTICAL SOLUTION:
    β̂ = (XᵀX + αI)⁻¹Xᵀy

HYPERPARAMETERS:
    • α = 10 (regularization strength)

CITATION: Hoerl & Kennard (1970). Ridge Regression: Biased Estimation for Nonorthogonal Problems.


----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
7. LASSO REGRESSION (L1 Regularization)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PREDICTION FUNCTION:
    ŷ = Xβ + β₀

LOSS FUNCTION (L1-Penalized Least Squares):
    L(β) = 1/(2N) ||y - Xβ||² + α||β||₁
         = 1/(2N) Σ(yᵢ - xᵢᵀβ)² + α Σ|βⱼ|

OPTIMIZATION (Coordinate Descent):
    No closed-form solution; solved iteratively via:

    β̂ⱼ = S(XⱼᵀRⱼ, Nα) / (XⱼᵀXⱼ)

    Where S(z, λ) = sign(z)(|z| - λ)₊ (soft thresholding)

FEATURE SELECTION:
    • Induces sparsity: some β̂ⱼ = 0 exactly
    • Automatic feature selection property

HYPERPARAMETERS:
    • α = 10 (regularization strength)

CITATION: Tibshirani (1996). Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso.


----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
8. ELASTIC NET (L1 + L2 Regularization)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PREDICTION FUNCTION:
    ŷ = Xβ + β₀

LOSS FUNCTION (Combined L1/L2 Penalty):
    L(β) = 1/(2N) ||y - Xβ||² + α[ρ||β||₁ + (1-ρ)/2 ||β||₂²]

    Where:
        • ρ = 0.5 (L1 ratio: 0=Ridge, 1=Lasso)
        • α = 10 (overall regularization strength)

OPTIMIZATION:
    Coordinate descent with elastic penalty.

ADVANTAGES:
    • Combines feature selection (L1) and coefficient shrinkage (L2)
    • Handles correlated features better than Lasso
    • More stable than Lasso when p >> n

HYPERPARAMETERS:
    • α = 10
    • l1_ratio = 0.5

CITATION: Zou & Hastie (2005). Regularization and Variable Selection via the Elastic Net.


----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
9. SUPPORT VECTOR REGRESSION (SVR)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PREDICTION FUNCTION (Kernel Trick):
    f(x) = Σ(i=1 to N) (αᵢ - αᵢ*)K(xᵢ, x) + b

Where:
    • K(xᵢ, x) = kernel function (RBF kernel used)
    • αᵢ, αᵢ* = dual variables (Lagrange multipliers)
    • b = bias term

KERNEL FUNCTION (RBF):
    K(xᵢ, xⱼ) = exp(-γ||xᵢ - xⱼ||²)

    Where γ = scale

LOSS FUNCTION (ε-insensitive):
    L_ε(y, f(x)) = max(0, |y - f(x)| - ε)

    No penalty for errors < ε (defines tube around regression line)

OPTIMIZATION (Primal Form):
    min  1/2||w||² + C Σ(ξᵢ + ξᵢ*)
    s.t. |yᵢ - f(xᵢ)| ≤ ε + ξᵢ

HYPERPARAMETERS:
    • C = 100 (regularization parameter)
    • γ = scale (kernel coefficient)
    • ε = 0.1 (epsilon in epsilon-SVR)

CITATION: Vapnik (1995). The Nature of Statistical Learning Theory.


----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
10. K-NEAREST NEIGHBORS REGRESSOR
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PREDICTION FUNCTION (Non-parametric):
    ŷ(x) = 1/K Σ(xᵢ∈N_K(x)) yᵢ

Where:
    • N_K(x) = K nearest neighbors of x in training set
    • K = 5
    • Distance metric: Euclidean d(xᵢ, xⱼ) = ||xᵢ - xⱼ||₂

WEIGHTED VARIANT:
    ŷ(x) = Σ(i=1 to K) wᵢyᵢ / Σ(i=1 to K) wᵢ

    Where wᵢ = 1/d(x, xᵢ) (inverse distance weighting)

COMPLEXITY:
    • Training: O(1) (stores all training data)
    • Prediction: O(Nd) for each query (can use KD-tree for O(log N))

HYPERPARAMETERS:
    • n_neighbors = 5
    • weights = 'uniform' (uniform or distance)
    • metric = 'euclidean'

CITATION: Fix & Hodges (1951). Discriminatory Analysis - Nonparametric Discrimination.


====================================================================================================
SECTION 3: NOTATION REFERENCE
====================================================================================================

COMMON NOTATION USED ACROSS ALL MODELS:

VARIABLES:
    • N = number of training samples = 3861
    • d = number of features = 28
    • xᵢ ∈ ℝᵈ = feature vector for sample i
    • yᵢ ∈ ℝ = target value for sample i
    • ŷᵢ = predicted value for sample i
    • X = [x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ]ᵀ ∈ ℝᴺˣᵈ = design matrix
    • y = [y₁, y₂, ..., yₙ]ᵀ ∈ ℝᴺ = target vector
    • β ∈ ℝᵈ = coefficient vector
    • β₀ ∈ ℝ = intercept term

LOSS FUNCTIONS:
    • MSE = 1/N Σ(yᵢ - ŷᵢ)² (Mean Squared Error)
    • RMSE = √MSE (Root Mean Squared Error)
    • MAE = 1/N Σ|yᵢ - ŷᵢ| (Mean Absolute Error)
    • R² = 1 - SS_res/SS_tot (Coefficient of Determination)
        Where:
            SS_res = Σ(yᵢ - ŷᵢ)² (residual sum of squares)
            SS_tot = Σ(yᵢ - ȳ)² (total sum of squares)

REGULARIZATION:
    • L1 norm: ||β||₁ = Σ|βⱼ| (Lasso penalty)
    • L2 norm: ||β||₂² = Σβⱼ² (Ridge penalty)
    • α, λ = regularization strength hyperparameters

GRADIENTS:
    • gᵢ = ∂L/∂ŷᵢ (first-order gradient)
    • hᵢ = ∂²L/∂ŷᵢ² (second-order gradient / Hessian)

ENSEMBLE NOTATION:
    • M, B, T, K = number of base models/trees
    • η = learning rate / shrinkage parameter
    • fₖ(x) = k-th base model output
    • wⱼ = leaf weight in tree-based models

EVALUATION METRICS REPORTED:
    • Test R² = 0.9810 (best model)
    • Test RMSE = 78892.87 (best model)
    • Test MAE = 51876.68 (best model)

DATA SPLIT:
    • Training: 60% = 3861 samples
    • Validation: 20% = 1287 samples
    • Testing: 20% = 1287 samples

====================================================================================================

Model Family Performance Summary:

     Model Family  Count  Best R²  Mean R²  Std R²  Representative Model
Gradient Boosting      5   0.9810   0.9696  0.0224     CatBoostRegressor
    Random Forest      2   0.9797   0.9731  0.0093   ExtraTreesRegressor
    Linear Models      7   0.9623   0.9139  0.0843         BayesianRidge
       Tree-based      1   0.9517   0.9517     NaN DecisionTreeRegressor
   Instance-based      1   0.9404   0.9404     NaN   KNeighborsRegressor
   Kernel Methods      3   0.0487  -0.7887  1.3913                   SVR

✅ MATHEMATICAL FORMULATION DOCUMENTATION COMPLETE
====================================================================================================

FILES GENERATED FOR TECHNICAL PAPER:
    • Console output above contains complete formulations
    • table_model_family_mathematical_summary.csv (family comparison)

RECOMMENDED USAGE IN PAPER:
    1. Methods Section: Include prediction functions + loss functions
    2. Appendix: Full algorithmic details (ordered boosting, leaf-wise growth, etc.)
    3. Tables: Hyperparameter values for reproducibility
    4. Notation: Reference Section 3 for symbol definitions

KEY CITATIONS TO INCLUDE:
    • CatBoost: Prokhorenkova et al. (2018) NeurIPS
    • XGBoost: Chen & Guestrin (2016) KDD
    • LightGBM: Ke et al. (2017) NeurIPS
    • Gradient Boosting: Friedman (2001) Annals of Statistics
    • Random Forest: Breiman (2001) Machine Learning
    • Lasso: Tibshirani (1996) JRSSB
    • Elastic Net: Zou & Hastie (2005) JRSSB
    • SVR: Vapnik (1995) Springer
